content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Python is often described as a language where everything is an object. Although this statement may seem simple, understanding what it truly means is essential for becoming a better Python programmer. Every value you create, whether it is an integer, a string, a list, a dictionary, a function, or even a class, is represented as an object in memory. Variables in Python do not store the actual values themselves; instead, they store references to these objects. This design makes Python powerful and flexible, but it can also lead to unexpected behavior if programmers do not understand how objects work.
One of the most common sources of confusion for beginners is why changing one variable sometimes changes another, or why modifying a list behaves differently from modifying an integer. The answers lie in understanding object identity, object types, mutability, and the way Python manages memory. Learning these concepts not only helps avoid common programming mistakes but also makes it easier to write cleaner, more efficient, and more predictable code.
Before discussing mutable and immutable objects, it is important to understand two built-in Python functions: type() and id(). The type() function tells us the type (or class) of an object, while the id() function returns the object's identity, which is a unique identifier for that object during its lifetime.
For example:
number = 89print(type(number))Output:
<class 'int'>The output shows that number is an integer object. The same idea applies to strings, lists, dictionaries, tuples, and many other data types.
name = "John"print(type(name))Output:
<class 'str'>Python also allows us to inspect an object’s identity using the id() function.
number = 89print(id(number))Possible output:
140706215641392The exact number will differ on every computer because object identities depend on the current Python session. The important point is that every object has its own unique identity while it exists.
An interesting situation occurs when two variables reference the same object.
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = aprint(id(a))
print(id(b))Possible output:
2154830590208
2154830590208Both variables have exactly the same identity. This means Python did not create two different lists. Instead, both a and b point to the same list in memory. You can visualize this relationship as follows:
a ───┐
│
▼
[1, 2, 3]
▲
│
b ───┘Since both variables reference the same object, any modification made through one variable will also be visible through the other.
This behavior becomes especially important when working with mutable objects. A mutable object is an object whose contents can be changed after it has been created. Some of the most common mutable objects in Python are lists, dictionaries, and sets. Instead of creating a completely new object every time a value changes, Python simply modifies the existing object.
For example, lists are mutable.
numbers = [1, 2, 3]numbers.append(4)print(numbers)Output:
[1, 2, 3, 4]The list has been updated without creating a new list. We can verify this by checking the object’s identity before and after the modification.
numbers = [1, 2, 3]print(id(numbers))numbers.append(4)print(id(numbers))Possible output:
2154830590208
2154830590208The identity remains exactly the same, proving that Python modified the original object rather than creating a new one.
Now consider another example.
a = [1, 2]b = ab.append(3)print(a)
print(b)Output:
[1, 2, 3]
[1, 2, 3]Many beginners expect only b to change, but both variables display the updated list because they reference the same object in memory. When append() is called, Python modifies that shared list instead of creating another one.
Dictionaries behave in exactly the same way because they are also mutable.
student = {
"name": "Alice",
"age": 20
}student["age"] = 21print(student)Output:
{'name': 'Alice', 'age': 21}The existing dictionary is updated directly, and its identity remains unchanged.
Sets are mutable as well.
languages = {"Python", "C"}languages.add("Java")print(languages)Possible output:
{'Python', 'C', 'Java'}Again, Python modifies the existing object instead of creating a new one.
Understanding mutable objects is extremely important because multiple variables can reference the same object. If one variable changes that object, every other variable pointing to it will immediately observe the same changes.
For example:
first = ["apple", "banana"]second = firstsecond.remove("banana")print(first)
print(second)Output:
['apple']
['apple']Although only second was modified, first also changed because both variables referenced the exact same list. This behavior often surprises new Python programmers, but it becomes much easier to understand once we remember that variables store references to objects rather than the objects themselves. Learning this concept provides the foundation for understanding how Python manages memory and prepares us to explore immutable objects and function arguments in the next sections.
Unlike mutable objects, immutable objects cannot be changed after they are created. If it seems that an immutable object has been modified, Python actually creates a completely new object and updates the variable to reference that new object. Some of the most common immutable objects in Python are integers, floating-point numbers, booleans, strings, bytes, and tuples.
Integers provide one of the simplest examples of immutable objects.
number = 10print(id(number))number += 1print(id(number))Possible output:
140704035922120
140704035922152The two object identities are different because Python created a new integer object with the value 11. The original object containing the value 10 still exists until Python's garbage collector removes it, but the variable number now references a different object.
The same behavior can be observed with strings.
name = "John"print(id(name))name += " Smith"print(name)
print(id(name))Possible output:
140704036140976
John Smith
140704036141488Although it appears that " Smith" was added to the original string, Python actually created a brand-new string object containing "John Smith" and updated the variable to point to it. The original string "John" was never modified.
Tuples are another example of immutable objects.
numbers = (1, 2, 3)print(numbers)Output:
(1, 2, 3)Unlike lists, tuples do not provide methods such as append() or remove(). Attempting to modify a tuple results in an error.
numbers = (1, 2, 3)numbers[0] = 5Output:
TypeError: 'tuple' object does not support item assignmentPython prevents changes to tuples because they are immutable.
The difference between mutable and immutable objects becomes much clearer when assigning variables.
Consider this example using integers.
a = 5
b = ab += 1print(a)
print(b)Output:
5
6Even though b was originally assigned the value of a, changing b does not affect a. This happens because integers are immutable. When b += 1 executes, Python creates a new integer object with the value 6 and makes b reference that new object. The variable a continues pointing to the original integer object containing 5.
Now compare this behavior with lists.
a = [5]
b = ab.append(6)print(a)
print(b)Output:
[5, 6]
[5, 6]Both variables now contain the same updated list because lists are mutable. Instead of creating a new object, Python modified the existing list that both variables reference.
Understanding this distinction is extremely important because it explains many situations that confuse beginners. Developers often expect assignment to create an independent copy of an object. However, assignment in Python simply creates another reference to the same object. Whether modifications affect other variables depends entirely on whether the object itself is mutable.
Another useful concept is the difference between the == operator and the is operator.
The == operator checks whether two objects contain equal values.
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = [1, 2, 3]print(a == b)Output:
TrueAlthough the lists contain identical values, they are different objects.
The is operator checks whether two variables reference the exact same object.
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = [1, 2, 3]print(a is b)Output:
FalseNow compare that with the following example.
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = aprint(a is b)Output:
TrueThis time, both variables reference exactly the same list object.
Understanding the difference between == and is is especially useful when debugging programs. Two variables may contain equal values without referring to the same object in memory.
At this point, we can summarize the key difference between mutable and immutable objects. Mutable objects allow their contents to be modified while keeping the same identity, whereas immutable objects never change after creation. Whenever an immutable object appears to change, Python creates a new object instead. This design improves performance, increases safety, and helps Python manage memory efficiently while giving programmers predictable behavior.
